items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.

items.q: What three Ps are added to make the extended marketing mix (7Ps)?
items.correct: People (staff/service quality), Process (how the service is delivered), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues — uniforms, packaging, branding) — added to make the 4Ps relevant to services marketing.
items.misconception: The 7Ps are just the 4Ps repeated with different words.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps include Profit, Planning and Performance.
items.why: The extended mix specifically addresses services, where customer experience depends on staff, process and atmospherics — not just product. Students treat 4Ps and 7Ps as interchangeable, missing what services marketing requires.

items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.
The answer examiners want is: Public sector. “Private sector” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Charity. “Private business” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A charity raises £4,000 and spends 75% on its cause. £ spent on cause?
items.why: The real figure is 3000. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
